title: GET-OAUTH — A Case Study in OAuth Token Exposure & Tenant Scoping
platform: Zernio.com (formerly getlate.dev at time of disclosure)
summary: Under certain tenant configurations, authenticated users received responses containing OAuth access_token and refresh_token values for all profiles in a tenant — including those outside their access scope.
disclosure: The following vulnerability was disclosed responsibly with the GETLATE organization. GETLATE acknowledged and promptly patched the issue and provided public vulnerability disclosure approval. This write-up is a technical case study and learning resource.

sections.heading: Summary
sections.body:
Under certain tenant configurations, authenticated users received web request responses containing data about all profiles within a tenant, even those outside their access scope.

These responses included OAuth `access_token` and `refresh_token` values in plaintext for connected social media accounts, allowing unauthorized third-party actions outside the platform's intended permission model.

sections.heading: Reproduction Steps
items: Create a new profile — connect a social account and do NOT scope any users to this profile. (Tested with Twitter/X.)
Log off from the tenant admin account.
Login as an end-user who is not scoped to the new profile.
In the main dashboard: open Dev Tools → navigate to the Network tab and refresh the dashboard.
Locate responses — find JSON files named `accounts`.
Inspect response — all tenant profiles appear, including those outside the user's scope.
Check metadata section — OAuth `access_token` and `refresh_token` are exposed in plaintext.

sections.heading: Impact
items: OAuth tokens exposed → direct third-party API access.
Users could post, delete, or modify content outside permissions even if access was revoked from the GETLATE platform.
Tokens remained valid until rotated or revoked.

timeline.text: Reported a related visual bug within the tenant where profiles a user was not scoped for were listed (but not accessible) in the new Inbox feature.
Visual bug patched; non-scoped profile OAuth vulnerability was partially mitigated through this as well.
Safe-haven granted; remaining vulnerability fully patched.

sections.heading: Remediation
items: Remove or protect OAuth tokens from frontend responses.
Only return resources scoped to the authenticated user.
Securely store OAuth credentials — never expose to clients.

sections.heading: Closing Thoughts
sections.body:
Small authorization gaps combined with exposed credentials can be critical. Cooperative disclosure and timely remediation are key to secure multi-tenant design.

Thank you to Miki and the GETLATE team for safe-haven for disclosure, prompt communication, prompt patching, and overall addressing this in a professional, responsible manner.
